name: Low Back Pain
summary: Acute/subacute axial or radicular lumbar pain; screen for red-flag etiologies (cauda equina, infection, malignancy, AAA, fracture) vs benign mechanical strain.

triage: Pain score, onset/duration, mechanism (atraumatic vs trauma)
Screen red flags: fever, IVDU, cancer hx, anticoagulation, incontinence, saddle anesthesia
Vital signs incl. temperature; assess for hemodynamic instability (AAA)
Ambulation/gait assessment
nursing: Position of comfort; assist transfers, fall precautions
Establish IV access only if parenteral meds or unstable*  if indicated
Bladder scan for retention if cauda equina suspected*  if indicated
Reassess pain after analgesia
keyOrders: Focused neuro exam: motor, sensory, reflexes, straight-leg raise
Digital rectal exam for tone/saddle sensation if cauda equina suspected*  if indicated
Post-void residual / bladder scan*  if indicated
Bedside AAA ultrasound if age >50 with risk factors*  if indicated
labs: Usually none for benign mechanical pain
CBC, ESR, CRP if infection/abscess suspected*  if indicated
Blood cultures if febrile/septic*  if indicated
UA if urinary symptoms or retention*  if indicated
imaging: No imaging for uncomplicated pain <6 weeks (ACEP/Choosing Wisely)
Plain lumbar films if trauma, osteoporosis, or suspected fracture*  if indicated
MRI lumbar spine STAT if cauda equina, epidural abscess, or cord compression*  if indicated
CT abdomen/aorta if AAA suspected*  if indicated
meds: Ibuprofen 600 mg PO q6h or ketorolac 15-30 mg IV
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO q6h
Cyclobenzaprine 5-10 mg PO TID PRN spasm
Diazepam 5 mg PO PRN severe spasm*  if indicated
Avoid routine opioids; oxycodone 5 mg PO if severe refractory pain*  if indicated
reassess: Re-evaluate pain and ambulation after analgesia
Repeat neuro exam if symptoms progress
Confirm ability to ambulate and void before discharge
disposition: Discharge home for uncomplicated mechanical pain once ambulatory
Transfer for emergent spine surgery/MRI if cauda equina or cord compression
Admit/transfer for IV antibiotics if epidural abscess/discitis
discharge: Stay active; avoid prolonged bed rest
Scheduled NSAID + acetaminophen; heat/ice
Strict return precautions: weakness, numbness, incontinence, fever
Primary care or spine follow-up in 1-2 weeks
followup: PCP in 1-2 weeks if not improving
Physical therapy referral for subacute pain
Spine/ortho referral for persistent radiculopathy

redFlags: Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention/incontinence, bilateral leg weakness (cauda equina)
Fever + back pain, IVDU, immunosuppression (epidural abscess/discitis)
Pulsatile mass, hypotension, tearing pain (AAA)
History of cancer, unexplained weight loss, night pain (malignancy)
Progressive neuro deficit or new bowel/bladder dysfunction
compliance: Provide EMTALA medical screening exam to exclude emergency conditions before discharge
Cauda equina/epidural abscess/AAA require stabilization and appropriate transfer to facility with neurosurgery/vascular and MRI capability
Document transfer acceptance, mode, and risk/benefit for time-sensitive spinal emergencies
Observation acceptable for pain control; transfer if surgical intervention or advanced imaging unavailable on site

name: Neck Pain
summary: Cervical pain ranging from benign strain to life-threats (C-spine fracture, cord injury, meningitis, vascular dissection); risk-stratify by mechanism and red flags.

triage: Mechanism: trauma vs atraumatic; high-risk MVC/fall/diving
Screen: fever/photophobia (meningitis), neuro deficit, dissection risk
Apply Canadian C-Spine Rule for trauma; maintain c-collar if indicated
Vital signs incl. temperature
nursing: Maintain spinal immobilization/c-collar until cleared if trauma
Position of comfort if atraumatic
IV access if parenteral meds or unstable*  if indicated
Reassess neuro status frequently
keyOrders: Detailed cervical and neuro exam (motor/sensory/reflexes)
Spinal precautions until clinically/radiographically cleared if trauma
Cardiac monitor if dissection/stroke concern*  if indicated
Apply Canadian C-Spine Rule to determine imaging need
labs: Usually none for mechanical pain
CBC, ESR, CRP, blood cultures if infection suspected*  if indicated
Coags/type & screen if traumatic and surgery possible*  if indicated
imaging: CT cervical spine if Canadian C-Spine Rule positive*  if indicated
CTA neck if dissection/vertebral injury suspected*  if indicated
MRI cervical spine if cord compression/myelopathy or epidural abscess*  if indicated
No imaging for low-risk atraumatic mechanical pain
meds: Ibuprofen 600 mg PO q6h or ketorolac 15-30 mg IV
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO q6h
Cyclobenzaprine 5-10 mg PO TID PRN spasm
Antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone 2 g IV) if meningitis suspected per protocol*  if indicated
reassess: Repeat neuro exam to detect progression
Reassess pain after analgesia and ROM
Confirm C-spine cleared before collar removal in trauma
disposition: Discharge for uncomplicated cervical strain
Transfer for neurosurgery if C-spine fracture, cord injury, or dissection
Admit/transfer for meningitis or epidural abscess management
discharge: Analgesia, gentle ROM, heat; avoid prolonged collar use
Return for weakness, numbness, severe headache, fever, fainting
Soft collar only short-term for comfort if needed
PCP follow-up 1 week
followup: PCP or spine clinic in 1 week
Physical therapy for persistent mechanical pain
Neurology/vascular follow-up if dissection treated

redFlags: Midline tenderness, focal neuro deficit, paresthesias post-trauma (C-spine/cord injury)
Fever, neck stiffness, photophobia, altered mental status (meningitis)
Horner syndrome, focal deficit, severe sudden pain (carotid/vertebral dissection)
Progressive weakness or gait disturbance (myelopathy/cord compression)
High-risk mechanism with distracting injury or intoxication
compliance: EMTALA MSE must exclude C-spine injury, cord compression, dissection, and CNS infection prior to disposition
Stabilize and arrange appropriate transfer to trauma/neurosurgical center for unstable C-spine or cord injury
Suspected meningococcal disease is a reportable condition to Texas DSHS
Document spinal immobilization and clearance; transfer if CT/MRI or specialty unavailable on site

name: Extremity Injury / Fracture
summary: Closed extremity trauma with suspected fracture/dislocation; assess neurovascular status, identify open injury, compartment syndrome, and high-risk fractures.

triage: Mechanism, time of injury, deformity, open wound check
Assess distal neurovascular status (pulses, cap refill, motor, sensation)
Pain score; immobilize and elevate affected limb
Remove rings/constricting jewelry early
nursing: Splint in position found; ice and elevate
IV access for analgesia if significant fracture*  if indicated
Document neurovascular exam pre- and post-splinting
NPO if reduction/sedation anticipated*  if indicated
keyOrders: Distal neurovascular assessment and documentation
Splint/immobilize affected extremity
Analgesia early; elevation and ice
Tetanus status review if any skin breach*  if indicated
labs: Usually none for isolated closed fracture
CBC, type & screen if operative or significant blood loss*  if indicated
Coags if anticoagulated or pre-op*  if indicated
CK if crush injury / compartment concern*  if indicated
imaging: Plain radiographs of injured area (include joint above and below)
Apply Ottawa Ankle/Knee/Foot Rules to guide imaging
CT for complex intra-articular, hip, or pelvic fractures*  if indicated
Vascular imaging/CTA if pulse deficit*  if indicated
meds: Ibuprofen 600 mg PO or ketorolac 15-30 mg IV
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO
Morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV for severe pain
Procedural sedation (ketamine/propofol) for reduction per protocol*  if indicated
Hematoma block / regional anesthesia for reduction*  if indicated
reassess: Repeat neurovascular exam after splinting/reduction
Post-reduction radiograph to confirm alignment
Reassess pain and perfusion before discharge
disposition: Discharge with splint/immobilization for stable, reduced fractures
Transfer to ortho/trauma for operative, open, or unstable fractures
Admit/transfer for hip fracture, multi-trauma, or NV compromise
discharge: Splint care, elevation, ice; non-weight-bearing as appropriate
Analgesia plan; crutches/sling and instruction
Return for increasing pain, numbness, pallor, or splint problems
Orthopedic follow-up within 3-7 days
followup: Orthopedics in 3-7 days for definitive casting/management
Sooner follow-up for fractures needing early operative planning
PCP for pain management coordination

redFlags: Absent or diminished distal pulses / cold pale limb (vascular injury)
Pain out of proportion, paresthesia, tense compartment (compartment syndrome)
Open wound over fracture (open fracture)
Gross deformity with neurologic deficit
Hip fracture in elderly or suspected pathologic fracture
compliance: EMTALA MSE includes neurovascular assessment and identification of open/limb-threatening injury before disposition
Stabilize (splint, analgesia, antibiotics if open) and arrange appropriate transfer to facility with orthopedic/operative capability
Document neurovascular status pre/post intervention and reason for transfer vs observation
Report suspected abuse if injury pattern inconsistent with history (Texas mandatory reporting)

name: Joint Pain / Swelling
summary: Mono/polyarticular joint pain with effusion; differentiate septic arthritis, crystal arthropathy (gout/pseudogout), inflammatory, and reactive causes.

triage: Number of joints, onset, fever, prior gout/RA history
Vital signs incl. temperature; SIRS screen
Assess joint for warmth, redness, effusion, ROM
Pain score and weight-bearing ability
nursing: Position of comfort, elevate affected joint, ice
IV access if febrile/septic or parenteral meds*  if indicated
Prepare for arthrocentesis (sterile tray)*  if indicated
Reassess after analgesia
keyOrders: Arthrocentesis for synovial fluid analysis if effusion present
Joint exam and documentation of effusion/warmth
Blood cultures if febrile before antibiotics*  if indicated
Mark/measure affected joint for serial exam
labs: Synovial fluid: cell count w/ diff, Gram stain, culture, crystals
CBC, ESR, CRP
Serum uric acid (limited acute value), BMP
Blood cultures if septic joint suspected*  if indicated
imaging: Plain radiograph of affected joint
Ultrasound to confirm/guide effusion aspiration*  if indicated
MRI if osteomyelitis or deep abscess suspected*  if indicated
meds: NSAID: indomethacin 50 mg PO TID or naproxen 500 mg PO BID (gout)
Colchicine 1.2 mg PO then 0.6 mg in 1h for gout*  if indicated
Prednisone 30-40 mg PO if NSAID contraindicated*  if indicated
Empiric vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV +/- ceftriaxone 2 g IV if septic joint*  if indicated
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO for adjunct analgesia
reassess: Review synovial fluid results to guide therapy
Reassess pain, ROM, and weight-bearing
Monitor for systemic toxicity/sepsis
disposition: Discharge for confirmed crystal/inflammatory arthritis once controlled
Admit/transfer for septic arthritis requiring drainage and IV antibiotics
Transfer to facility with orthopedics if joint washout needed
discharge: Disease-specific meds (e.g., colchicine/NSAID taper for gout)
Rest, elevation, ice; activity as tolerated
Return for fever, spreading redness, inability to move joint
Rheumatology/PCP follow-up 1 week
followup: PCP/rheumatology in 1 week for chronic management
Urgent ortho follow-up if septic joint treated
Urate-lowering therapy discussion after acute gout resolves

redFlags: Fever + hot swollen joint with restricted motion (septic arthritis)
Synovial WBC >50,000 or positive Gram stain
Rapidly progressive joint destruction or systemic toxicity
Prosthetic joint involvement
Immunocompromised host with monoarthritis
compliance: EMTALA MSE must exclude septic arthritis (joint-threatening emergency) before disposition
Septic arthritis requires stabilization, empiric antibiotics, and appropriate transfer to facility with orthopedic surgery for washout
Document arthrocentesis findings and transfer rationale; observation acceptable for crystal arthropathy
Gonococcal arthritis: report reportable STI to Texas DSHS if confirmed

name: Sprain / Strain
summary: Soft-tissue ligament (sprain) or muscle/tendon (strain) injury; exclude fracture via decision rules and assess for severe/grade III injury.

triage: Mechanism, time, ability to bear weight
Assess swelling, ecchymosis, deformity, point tenderness
Neurovascular check distal to injury
Pain score; apply RICE measures
nursing: Rest, ice, compression, elevation (RICE)
Apply elastic wrap or splint for comfort/stability
Reassess pain after analgesia
Provide crutches/sling as needed
keyOrders: Apply Ottawa Ankle/Foot/Knee Rules to determine imaging need
Distal neurovascular assessment
Immobilize/support injured area
Early analgesia and elevation
labs: None routinely required
imaging: Radiographs only if Ottawa rules positive or fracture suspected*  if indicated
No imaging for clear low-grade sprain meeting rule-out criteria
Ultrasound/MRI outpatient for suspected complete tendon rupture*  if indicated
meds: Ibuprofen 600 mg PO q6h or naproxen 500 mg PO BID
Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO q6h
Topical diclofenac gel for localized pain*  if indicated
reassess: Confirm ability to bear weight/ambulate before discharge
Reassess neurovascular status after splinting
Verify pain controlled
disposition: Discharge home in nearly all cases
Refer to ortho if complete rupture or grade III instability
Transfer rarely needed unless associated fracture/NV injury
discharge: RICE for 48-72h, then gradual mobilization
Supportive brace/wrap; weight-bear as tolerated
Return for inability to bear weight, numbness, increasing swelling
PCP/sports med follow-up if not improving in 5-7 days
followup: PCP or sports medicine in 1 week if persistent
Physical therapy for functional rehab
Orthopedics for suspected complete ligament/tendon rupture

redFlags: Inability to bear weight with bony tenderness (occult fracture)
Gross joint instability suggesting complete ligament rupture
Neurovascular compromise distal to injury
Significant hemarthrosis or rapidly expanding swelling
Achilles/quadriceps tendon rupture (palpable gap, positive Thompson)
compliance: EMTALA MSE to exclude fracture, NV injury, and complete tendon/ligament rupture before discharge
Most cases stabilized and discharged; transfer only if associated emergency injury identified
Document neurovascular exam and decision-rule application
Report suspected abuse if mechanism inconsistent with injury (Texas mandatory reporting)

name: Joint Dislocation
summary: Displaced joint articulation requiring prompt reduction; assess neurovascular status, associated fracture, and post-reduction stability.

triage: Mechanism, joint involved, deformity, time since injury
Assess distal neurovascular status (critical, e.g., axillary nerve, popliteal artery)
Pain score; immobilize in position found
Identify high-risk dislocation (knee, hip) needing urgent action
nursing: Immobilize/support affected joint, ice
IV access for procedural sedation/analgesia
NPO and pre-sedation assessment if reduction planned
Continuous monitoring during sedation (SpO2, ETCO2, BP)
keyOrders: Pre- and post-reduction neurovascular documentation
Pre-reduction radiograph (unless emergent threat)
Procedural sedation setup and monitoring
Cardiac monitor and pulse oximetry during sedation
labs: Usually none for isolated dislocation
Pre-sedation labs/pregnancy test per protocol*  if indicated
Type & screen if operative or vascular injury*  if indicated
imaging: Pre-reduction plain radiographs (AP/lateral, joint-specific views)
Post-reduction radiographs to confirm relocation
CTA if knee dislocation or pulse deficit (popliteal artery)*  if indicated
CT for complex fracture-dislocation*  if indicated
meds: Procedural sedation: ketamine 1-2 mg/kg IV, propofol 0.5-1 mg/kg IV, or etomidate
Fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV +/- midazolam 0.02 mg/kg IV
Intra-articular lidocaine as alternative for shoulder reduction*  if indicated
Post-reduction analgesia: ibuprofen 600 mg PO / acetaminophen 1000 mg PO
reassess: Immediate post-reduction neurovascular re-exam
Confirm reduction radiographically
Reassess sedation recovery before discharge
disposition: Discharge after successful reduction, immobilization, and stable NV exam
Transfer if irreducible, open, or vascular injury (esp. knee dislocation)
Admit/transfer for hip dislocation or fracture-dislocation needing OR
discharge: Immobilize (sling/splint/brace) per joint
Analgesia plan; activity restrictions
Return for recurrent dislocation, numbness, pallor, severe pain
Orthopedic follow-up within 3-7 days
followup: Orthopedics in 3-7 days for stability assessment/rehab
Vascular surgery follow-up if knee dislocation with vascular monitoring
Physical therapy for shoulder/patellar rehab

redFlags: Absent distal pulse / cold limb (vascular injury, esp. knee dislocation)
Neurologic deficit (axillary, peroneal, sciatic nerve)
Open dislocation or associated displaced fracture
Irreducible dislocation after attempts
Hip dislocation (risk of avascular necrosis - time-sensitive)
compliance: EMTALA MSE includes neurovascular assessment and reduction attempt within capability before transfer
Knee dislocation mandates vascular evaluation; stabilize and transfer to vascular/ortho center if compromise
Document pre/post-reduction NV exam, sedation monitoring, and transfer rationale
Procedural sedation must follow facility credentialing and monitoring standards

name: Open Fracture
summary: Fracture with overlying skin/soft-tissue breach communicating with bone; orthopedic emergency requiring early antibiotics, tetanus, and urgent surgical debridement.

triage: Identify open wound over fracture; control hemorrhage
Assess distal neurovascular status
ATLS approach if high-energy/polytrauma; vital signs
Note time of injury and contamination level (Gustilo grade)
nursing: Apply sterile saline-moistened dressing; do NOT repeatedly probe
Establish 2 large-bore IVs; control bleeding with pressure
Photograph wound once, then cover (limit repeated exposure)
Splint/immobilize; tetanus and antibiotic administration
keyOrders: Early IV antibiotics within 1 hour (time-critical)
Tetanus prophylaxis update
Sterile dressing, splinting, gross contamination removal
IV access, analgesia, NPO for OR
Neurovascular documentation pre/post splinting
labs: CBC, BMP, coags, type & crossmatch
Lactate if significant blood loss/polytrauma*  if indicated
Wound/bone cultures per ortho (often intraop)*  if indicated
imaging: Plain radiographs of injured limb (joint above and below)
CT for complex/intra-articular or pelvic involvement*  if indicated
CTA if vascular injury suspected*  if indicated
Trauma imaging per ATLS if polytrauma*  if indicated
meds: Cefazolin 2 g IV (Gustilo I-II); add gentamicin/piperacillin-tazobactam for III
Add penicillin/metronidazole for farm/soil contamination
Tetanus toxoid (Tdap) +/- tetanus immune globulin per status
Analgesia: morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV or fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV
reassess: Repeat neurovascular exam after splinting
Monitor hemodynamics and hemorrhage control
Confirm antibiotic and tetanus given and documented
disposition: Emergent transfer to facility with orthopedic surgery / OR for debridement
Do not delay transfer for non-essential workup
Activate trauma transfer protocol for polytrauma
discharge: Discharge not appropriate from FSED; requires operative care
Communicate antibiotics/tetanus given to receiving team
Ensure copies of imaging accompany transfer
followup: Definitive orthopedic surgical management at receiving facility
Trauma/ortho follow-up per accepting hospital
Infection surveillance post-debridement

redFlags: Active arterial bleeding or absent distal pulses
Gross contamination / Gustilo grade III injury
Neurologic deficit distal to fracture
Signs of shock or associated polytrauma
Delay >1 hour to antibiotics or >6-24h to debridement
compliance: EMTALA: provide MSE, stabilize (antibiotics, tetanus, hemorrhage control, splinting), then effect appropriate transfer to orthopedic/operative facility
FSED lacks OR capability for debridement - document medical necessity and risk/benefit of transfer
Use highest level of care during transfer; send records, imaging, and meds given
Report suspected abuse/assault if mechanism inconsistent (Texas mandatory reporting)

name: Suspected Compartment Syndrome
summary: Elevated intracompartmental pressure compromising perfusion (often post-fracture/crush/reperfusion); limb-threatening surgical emergency requiring urgent fasciotomy.

triage: Identify mechanism: fracture, crush, reperfusion, tight cast, burn
Assess for pain out of proportion and pain with passive stretch
Check 5 P's: pain, paresthesia, pallor, pulselessness (late), paralysis
Remove constricting casts/dressings immediately
nursing: Place limb at heart level (NOT elevated)
Remove circumferential dressings/splints/casts
Establish IV access; aggressive fluids for rhabdomyolysis
Frequent serial neurovascular checks; NPO for OR
keyOrders: Bivalve/remove constricting cast or dressing STAT
Compartment pressure measurement (Stryker) if available*  if indicated
Serial neurovascular exams documented with times
IV access, analgesia, NPO; keep limb at heart level
labs: CK (rhabdomyolysis), BMP (potassium), creatinine
CBC, coags, type & screen
Urinalysis for myoglobinuria; lactate*  if indicated
imaging: Plain radiographs to identify associated fracture
Imaging should not delay surgical decompression
CTA if concurrent vascular injury suspected*  if indicated
meds: Analgesia: fentanyl 1 mcg/kg IV or morphine 0.1 mg/kg IV (note: may mask pain)
IV crystalloid (NS/LR) for rhabdomyolysis-targeted urine output
Avoid limb elevation and avoid vasoconstrictors
Treat hyperkalemia per protocol if rhabdomyolysis*  if indicated
reassess: Serial compartment/neurovascular checks every 30-60 min
Trend CK and renal function
Diagnosis is clinical - do not wait for pressure if exam compelling
disposition: Emergent transfer to facility with orthopedic/vascular surgery for fasciotomy
Time-critical: fasciotomy ideally within 6 hours
Do not delay transfer for confirmatory testing
discharge: Discharge inappropriate; requires emergent operative decompression
Communicate pressure readings, CK, and exam to receiving surgeon
Transfer with continuous monitoring and IV access
followup: Operative fasciotomy and wound management at receiving facility
Renal monitoring for rhabdomyolysis-induced AKI
Orthopedic follow-up post-decompression

redFlags: Pain out of proportion and pain with passive stretch (earliest signs)
Tense, firm compartment on palpation
Paresthesia / sensory deficit progressing to paralysis
Compartment pressure >30 mmHg or delta pressure <30 mmHg
Pulselessness/pallor (late, ominous findings)
compliance: EMTALA: stabilize (relieve constriction, fluids, analgesia) and emergent appropriate transfer to surgical facility - limb-threatening emergency
FSED lacks fasciotomy capability - document time-critical necessity and risk/benefit of transfer
Document serial exams, pressures, and times; use highest-level transport
Report suspected abuse if crush mechanism inconsistent with history (Texas mandatory reporting)

name: Septic Joint
summary: Bacterial infection of a joint space (commonly S. aureus, gonococcal); joint-threatening emergency requiring urgent arthrocentesis, antibiotics, and surgical drainage.

triage: Assess hot, swollen, painful joint with restricted ROM
Vital signs incl. temperature; SIRS/sepsis screen
Risk factors: prosthetic joint, IVDU, immunosuppression, recent procedure
Pain score; immobilize joint
nursing: IV access; draw blood cultures before antibiotics
Immobilize and support affected joint
Prepare sterile arthrocentesis tray
Sepsis bundle if septic physiology (fluids, monitoring)
keyOrders: Arthrocentesis for synovial fluid analysis (before antibiotics if no delay)
Blood cultures x2 before antibiotics
IV access, IV fluids; cardiac monitor if septic
Empiric IV antibiotics after cultures
Immobilize joint
labs: Synovial fluid: cell count w/ diff, Gram stain, culture, crystals
CBC, ESR, CRP, BMP, lactate
Blood cultures x2
Procalcitonin*  if indicated; GC/chlamydia NAAT if gonococcal suspected*  if indicated
imaging: Plain radiograph of affected joint
Ultrasound to confirm effusion and guide aspiration*  if indicated
MRI if osteomyelitis or prosthetic/deep joint involvement*  if indicated
meds: Vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV (MRSA coverage)
Plus ceftriaxone 2 g IV (or cefepime if GNR/immunocompromised)
Ceftriaxone 1 g IV if gonococcal arthritis suspected
IV crystalloid 30 mL/kg if septic per Surviving Sepsis
Analgesia: acetaminophen 1000 mg PO; opioids for severe pain*  if indicated
reassess: Trend vitals and lactate; sepsis reassessment
Review synovial fluid Gram stain/cell count
Reassess pain and perfusion before/while arranging transfer
disposition: Admit/transfer to facility with orthopedics for surgical drainage/washout
Initiate sepsis management and antibiotics before transfer
ICU-level transfer if septic shock
discharge: Discharge inappropriate; requires admission and operative drainage
Communicate cultures, synovial results, and antibiotics given to receiving team
Transfer with IV access and monitoring
followup: Inpatient orthopedic surgical drainage and IV antibiotics
Infectious disease consultation for tailored therapy
Source/STI partner follow-up if gonococcal

redFlags: Hot swollen joint with fever and inability to bear weight/move joint
Synovial WBC >50,000 or positive Gram stain
Prosthetic joint infection
Sepsis/septic shock physiology (hypotension, tachycardia, elevated lactate)
Rapidly progressive joint destruction
compliance: EMTALA: provide MSE, stabilize (antibiotics, fluids, sepsis bundle), then appropriate transfer to facility with orthopedic surgery
FSED lacks operative washout capability - document medical necessity and risk/benefit of transfer/admission
Initiate Surviving Sepsis bundle measures if septic; send cultures and meds-given with patient
Gonococcal arthritis is a reportable STI to Texas DSHS
